content:
1.1 Information is Bits + Context # Important Concepts # Bytes are chunks of 8 bits Source files ( programs written by human) are stored as sequences of bytes. Most computers represent text characters using the ASCII standard that maps each character to a unique byte-size value. Text files: files consists exclusively of ASCII characters Binary files: All other files Information is stored as bytes in computers. The same bytes can be interpreted in different ways, which depends on the context. So, Information = Bits + Context
All information in a system—including disk files, programs stored in memory, user data stored in memory, and data transferred across a network—is represented as a bunch of bits. The only thing that distinguishes different data objects is the context in which we view them. For example, in different contexts, the same sequence of bytes might represent an integer, floating-point number, character string, or machine instruc
1.2 Programs Are Translated by Other Programs into Different Forms # Important Concepts # The source program is a text file that is written by human, which can be read and understood in that form. However, in order to ensure that the computer can understand the source file, the source file is translated by a compiler driver into a sequence of low-level machine-language instructions. These instructions are packaged into executable object program, which is a binary file.
Processing Phase : The source file is modified by a compiler driver into a modified source program. In this phase, the commands that begin with the ‘#’ character are modified. For example, the header files and macro definitions are replaced by actual contents.
Compilation Phase : The modified source program is compiled into an assembly program. The compiler translates the modified source program into an assembly-language program, which is a textual representation of low level machine instructions.
Side Note:
This phase is extremely useful because it produces the assembly program. Because the assembly program describes low-level machine-language instructions, this is the common output of different high-level programming languages, such as C, C++, Java, Rust, Python, etc. So, developers can use different programming languages to exploit their unique features when developing software. In a nutshell, the assembly bridge is the bridge that connects different high-level programming languages. However, there might be issues when bridging. For example, when commands in different languages corresponds to different machine-level behaviours, such as memory allocation, developers need to fix the issues by using the inter-process communication (IPC) or isolating machine-level instructions into different modules.
Assembly Phase : In this phase, the assembler translates the assembly program into machine-level instructions and packages them into a relocatable object program. The program is a binary file.
Linking Phase : If the program involves any standard C library, the linker merges precompiled object files with the program, creating an executable object file.
1.3 Is Pays to Understand How Compilation Systems Work # Important Concepts # Optimizing program performance: In order to optimize program performance, programmers need to get an insight into how high-level commands are translated into machine-level instructions and executed by computers. Understanding link-time errors: When building large software systems, link-time errors often occur. Avoiding security holes: Programmers need to understand the consequences of the way data and control information are stored on the program stack in order to deal with security holes, such as buffer overflow vulnerabilities. 1.4 Processors Read and Interpret Instructions Stored in Memory # Important Concepts # This is called the Von Neumann Architecture, a fundamental computer architecture used by most computers nowadays.
1.4.1 Hardware Organization of a System # Buses : A collection of electrical conduits running through the system that carry bytes of information back and forth between the components. It is designed to transfer fixed-size chunks of bytes known as words.
I/O Devices : Input/output (I/O) device is connected to the I/O busby either a controller or an adapter. Controller are chip sets in the device itself or on the system’s main printed circuit board (motherboard). An adapter is a card that plugs into a slot on the motherboard.
Main Memory : The main memory is a temporary storage device that holds both a program and the data is manipulates while the processor is executing the program. It is consist of a collection of dynamic random access memory (DRAM) chips. It is organized as a linear array of bytes with its own array index starting at zero.
Processor : the central processing unit (CPU), or processor, is the engine that interprets or executes instructions stored in main memory. Register is a word-size storage device called the program counter (PC). The register file is a small storage device that consists of a collection of word-size registers, each with its own unique name. The arithmetic/logic unit (ALU) computes new data and address values.
1.4.2 Running the hello Program # 1.5 Caches Matter # It is time consuming for a system to move information from one place to another when executing programs. These figures show how a program is executed by the system. First, it is loaded from the disk into main memory through the I/O bridge.
Larger and cheaper storage devices are usually slower, and expensive storage devices are usually faster. Because main memory is large and cheap, so it takes the CPU a lot of clock cycles to load data from main memory. This is known as the processor-memory gap. In order to deal with this, cache memory is introduced.
1.6 Storage Devices Form a Hierarchy # From top to bottom:
expensive => cheap faster => slower smaller => larger ==The main idea of a memory hierarchy is that storage at one level serves as a cache for a cache at the next lower level== Programmers exploit their understanding of the entire memory hierarchy to improve performance.
1.7 The Operating System Manages the Hardware # Operating system is a software between the application program and the hardware.
Purpose of the operating system:
to protect the hardware from misuse by runaway applications to provide applications with simple and uniform mechanisms for manipulating complicated and often wildly different low-level hardware devices. The operating system achieves both goals via the fundamental abstractions
1.7.1 Processes # The operating system provides the illusion that the program is the only one running on the system through the notion of a process.
For applications programs, they appear to have:
exclusive use of the processor, main memory, and I/O devices. the processor executes instructions in the program one after another, without interruption. Concurrency: the instructions of one process are interleaved with the instructions of another process. Each appears to have exclusive use of the hardware.
Concurrency is achieved by context switching.
Context is the state information that the process needs in order to run, which includes the current values of the PC, the register file, and the contents of main memory.
Context Switch is the act of saving the context of the current process, restoring the context of the new process, and then passing control to the new process.
When switching, the previous context is stored in kernel space.
User Space : stores application programs - Ring 3 in x86 Kernel Space : The kernel is a portion of the operating system code that is always resident in memory. It has the highest permission - Ring 0 in x86.
User programs execute system call instruction, transferring control to the kernel, when they want to perform system-level actions, such as read or write a file.
Kernel is NOT a separate process. It is the code and data structure the system uses to manage all the processes.
1.7.2 Threads # A process can consist of multiple execution units called threads.
Threads are important for concurrency in network servers because it is easier to share data between multiple threads than between multiple processes.
Besides, threads are more efficient than processes.
I would rather call processes the namespace for threads. This is similar to the idea of docker. In docker, container is like a namespace that provides separate resources and permissions for different images to run.
One way to make programs run faster : multi-threading when multiple processors are available.
Why need multiple processors:
Because if multi-threading with a single processor, the processor achieves multi-threading through context switch, so there isn’t a big difference in the performance. Multi-threading on multiple processors can improve performance because of parallelism.
1.7.3 Virtual Memory # Virtual memory is an abstraction that provides each process with the illusion that it have exclusive use of the main memory. Virtual address space is the same uniform view of memory for each process.
Areas in virtual address space: 1.7.4 Files # File is a sequence of bytes.
I/O device, disks, keyboards, displays, and networks are modeled as a file.
File is powerful because it provides applications with a unformed view for all the varied hardware components of a system.
1.8 Systems Communicate with Other Systems Using Networks # 1.9 Important Themes # An important idea to take away from this discussion is that a system is more than just hardware. It is a collection of intertwined hardware and systems software that must cooperate in order to achieve the ultimate goal of running application program
1.9.1 Amdahl’s Law # When we speed up one part of a system, the effect on the overall system performance depend on both how significant this part was and how much it sped up.
\(T_{\text{old}}\) is the time for a system to execute some application. \(\alpha\) is the fraction of \(T_{\text{old}}\) which a part of the system requires. If we improve the part’s performance by a factor of \(k\).
The overall execution time would thus be: $$T_{\text{new}} = (1 - \alpha)T_{\text{old}} + (\alpha T_{\text{old}}) / k = T_{\text{old}}[(1 - \alpha) + \alpha / k]$$From this, we can compute the speedup \(S = \frac {T_{\text{old}}} {T_{\text{new}}}\) as
$$S = \frac{1}{(1 - \alpha) + \alpha / k}$$ \(Solution\): \(A.\) $$ \alpha = \frac {1500km} {2500km} = 0.6 $$ $$ k = \frac {150km/hr} {100km/hr} = 1.5 $$ $$ \therefore S = \frac {1} {(1 - \alpha) + \frac {\alpha} {k}} = \frac {1} {(1-0.6) + \frac {0.6} {1.5}} = \frac {1} {0.4 + 0.4} = \frac {1} {0.8} = 1.25 $$ \(B.\) $$ \alpha = 0.6 $$ $$ S = 1.67 $$ $$ \because S = \frac {1} {(1-\alpha) + \frac {\alpha} {k}} $$ $$ \therefore k = \frac {\alpha} {\frac {1} {S} - (1-\alpha)} = \frac{0.6}{0.6 - 0.4} = \frac {0.6} {0.2} = 3 $$ $$ \therefore v_{\text{new}} = v_{\text{old}} \times k = 100km/hr \times 3 = 300km/hr $$ \(Solution:\) $$ S = 2 $$ $$ \alpha = 80\% = 0.8 $$ $$ \because S = \frac {1} {(1 - \alpha) + \frac {\alpha} {k}} $$ $$ \therefore k = \frac {\alpha} {\frac {1} {S} - (1 - \alpha)} = \frac {0.8} {0.5 - 0.2} = \frac {0.8} {0.3} = 2.67 $$ An interesting special case of Amdahl’s Law: $$ S_{\infty} = \lim_{k \to \infty} \frac{1}{(1 - \alpha) + \frac{\alpha}{k}} = \frac{1}{1 - \alpha} $$ 1.9.2 Concurrency and Parallelism # For digital computers :
we want them to do more we want them to run faster When the processor does more things at once, both of these two aspects improve.
Concurrency: A system with multiple, simultaneous activities. Parallelism : the use of concurrency to make a system run faster.
Thread-Level Concurrency # Uniprocessor System : a system consisting of a single processor switching among multiple tasks. Multiprocessor System : s system consisting of multiple processors all under a single kernel.
The difference between a uniprocessor system and a multiprocessor system. Multi-core Processor For each CPU core:
L1 data cache (d-cache) , L1 instruction cache (i-cache) L2 cache Shared among different cores:
L3 cache Main memory and disk Hyperthreading (simultaneous multi-threading) allows a single CPU to execute multiple glows of control.
It involves:
having multiple copies of some of the CPU hardware (program counters, register file) having only single copies of other parts of the hardware (ALU) This enables the processor to decide which of its threads to execute on a cycle-by-cycle basis, taking better advantage of its processing resources.
How multiprocessing improves system performance:
reduces the need to simulate concurrency when performing multiple tasks can run a single application program faster, but only if that program is expressed in terms of multiple threads that can effectively execute in parallel. Instruction-Level Parallelism # Instruction-Level Parallelism: modern processors can execute multiple instructions at one time.
Pipelining : the actions required to execute an instruction are partitioned into different steps and the processor hardware is organized as a series of stages which can operate in parallel, each performing one of these steps.
Single-Instruction, Multiple-Data (SIMD) Parallelism # SIMD Parallelism : a single instruction causes multiple operations to be performed in parallel (needs special hardware).
It is mostly used to speed up applications that process image, sound, and video data.
1.9.3 The Importance of Abstractions in Computer Systems # A major theme in computer systems is to provide abstract representations at different levels to hide the complexity of the actual implementations.
On the processor side: the instruction set architecture provides an abstraction of the actual processor hardware.
On the operating system side:
files as an abstraction of I/O devices virtual memory as an abstraction of program memory processes as an abstraction of a running program. the virtual machine as an abstraction of the entire computer, including the operating system, the processor, and the programs. 1.10 Summary #
title: CSAPP Chapter 1 Notes

content:
Link :https://leetcode.com/problems/reverse-words-in-a-string/description/?envType=study-plan-v2&envId=leetcode-75　Original Code # class Solution: def reverseWords(self, s: str) -> str: ans = [] tmp = [] s = list(s) n = len(s) i = 0 while i < n: if s[i] == ' ' and tmp != []: ans.append(''.join(tmp)) tmp = [] i += 1 elif s[i] != ' ': tmp.append(s[i]) i += 1 else: i += 1 if s[n-1] != ' ': ans.append(''.join(tmp)) ans.reverse() m = len(ans) j = 0 output = '' while j < m: if j != 0: output += ' ' output += ''.join(ans[j]) j += 1 return output Optimized Code # class Solution: def reverseWords(self, s: str) -> str: return ' '.join(s.split()[::-1]) Time : O(N) Space : O(N)
C++ Solution
class Solution { public: string reverseWords(string s) { reverse(s.begin(), s.end()); int n = s.size(); int l = 0; int r = 0; int i = 0; while (i < n) { while (i < n && s[i] == ' ') i++; if (i == n) break; while (i < n && s[i] != ' ') { s[r++] = s[i++]; } reverse(s.begin() + l, s.begin() + r); s[r++] = ' '; l = r; i++; } s.resize(r - 1); return s; } }; This is inspired by a community solution. It utilizes two pointers and only traverses the string once. By first reversing the whole string then reversing individual words in a single traversal, the solution manages to reverse the order of the words in time O(N) and space O(1)
Reflections # Choosing the right APIs to do the job is important in writing python code.
title: 151. Reverse Words in a String

content:
Link :　https://leetcode.com/problems/reverse-vowels-of-a-string/description/?envType=study-plan-v2&envId=leetcode-75
Original Code # class Solution: def reverseVowels(self, s: str) -> str: s = list(s) vowels = set("aeiouAEIOU") l = 0 r = len(s) - 1 while l < r: while l < r and s[l] not in vowels: l += 1 while l < r and s[r] not in vowels: r -= 1 s[l], s[r] = s[r], s[l] l += 1 r -= 1 return "".join(s) Optimized Code # The code above is good enough.
Reflections # Since string is immutable in python, converting string to list and doing list operations is much more straightforward and efficient.
title: 345. Reverse Vowels of a String

content:
Link :　https://leetcode.com/problems/greatest-common-divisor-of-strings/description/?envType=study-plan-v2&envId=leetcode-75
Original Code # class Solution: def canPlaceFlowers(self, flowerbed: List[int], n: int) -> bool: N = len(flowerbed) cnt = 0 for i in range(N): if flowerbed[i] == 0: flag1 = False flag2 = False if (i - 1 < 0 ) or (i - 1 >= 0 and flowerbed[i-1] != 1): flag1 = True if (i + 1 >= N) or (i + 1 < N and flowerbed[i+1] != 1): flag2 = True if(not flag1 or not flag2): continue else: cnt += 1 flowerbed[i] = 1 if cnt >= n: return True else: return False Optimized Code # class Solution: def canPlaceFlowers(self, flowerbed: List[int], n: int) -> bool: bed = [0] + flowerbed + [0] cnt = 0 i = 1 length = len(bed) while i < length - 1: if bed[i] == 0: if bed[i-1] == 0 and bed[i+1] == 0: cnt += 1 bed[i] = 1 if cnt >= n: return True i += 2 else: i += 1 else: i += 2 return cnt >= n Reflections # In order to utilize the branch prediction in cpu, I need to simplify the if-statements. I can also improve the time complexity of my code by avoiding unnecessary traversals based on the context. For example, because the the no-adjacent-flowers rule in the problem, I can skip a spot once finding flowerbed[i] == 1
title: 605. Can Place Flowers

content:
Link :　https://leetcode.com/problems/greatest-common-divisor-of-strings/description/?envType=study-plan-v2&envId=leetcode-75
Original Code # class Solution: def gcdOfStrings(self, str1: str, str2: str) -> str: length1, length2 = len(str1), len(str2) n = min(length1, length2) ans = '' i = 1 while i <= n: if length1 % i != 0 or length2 % i != 0: i += 1 continue tmp1 = str1[:i] tmp2 = str2[:i] if tmp1 != tmp2: i += 1 continue a = length1 // i b = length2 // i res1 = '' res2 = '' for _ in range(a): res1 += tmp1 for _ in range(b): res2 += tmp1 if (res1 != str1 or res2 != str2): i += 1 continue ans = tmp1 i += 1 return ans Optimized Code # class Solution: def gcdOfStrings(self, str1: str, str2: str) -> str: if(str1 + str2 != str2 + str1): return '' length1, length2 = len(str1), len(str2) n = min(length1, length2) gcd = 1 for i in range(1, n + 1): if(length1 % i == 0 and length2 % i == 0): gcd = i ans = str1[:gcd] return ans Reflections # The optimized code transforms the problem from a string operation problem to a numerical problem. This reduces unnecessary string operations, which reduces time and space complexity.
I should always think before writing the code.
title: 1071. Greatest Common Divisor of Strings

content:
Link :　https://leetcode.com/problems/kids-with-the-greatest-number-of-candies/description/?envType=study-plan-v2&envId=leetcode-75
Original Code # class Solution: def kidsWithCandies(self, candies: List[int], extraCandies: int) -> List[bool]: length = len(candies) Max = max(candies) ans : List[bool] = [] for i in range(length): if candies[i] + extraCandies >= Max: ans.append(True) else: ans.append(False) return ans Optimized Code # The code above is already good enough. Reflections # I should avoid unnecessary traversals on arrays, lists, graph, tree…
title: 1431. Kids With the Greatest Number of Candies

content:
Link :　https://leetcode.com/problems/merge-strings-alternately/description/? envType=study-plan-v2&envId=leetcode-75
Original Code # def mergeAlternately(self, word1: str, word2: str) -> str: length1 = len(word1) length2 = len(word2) n = min(length1, length2) ans = '' for i in range(n): ans+= word1[i] ans+= word2[i] if length1 > length2: ans += word1[n : length1] return ans else: ans += word2[n : length2] return ans Optimized Code # class Solution: def mergeAlternately(self, word1: str, word2: str) -> str: ans = [] n = min(len(word1), len(word2)) for i in range(n): ans.append(word1[i]) ans.append(word2[i]) ans.append(word1[n:]) ans.append(word2[n:]) return "".join(ans) Reflections # In python, string operation ‘a += b’ creates a copy of a string b and then append it to the end of string a because String Type is immutable. However, a list mutable, so python doesn’t need to create a copy, which reduces time complexity and space complexity.
title: 1768. Merge Strings Alternately
